status: MACHINE-PREPARED — NOT APPLIED. No transform value in data/specimens.json is changed by this file.
semantics: Proposed transform recalibration. Each row carries the archive's filed value, an independent blind score, and (for the largest gaps) an adjudicated ruling. The owner rules; accepted rows are applied by a separate explicit pass.

method.blind_scorer: claude-haiku, 27 agents x 25 face cards, filed value withheld, deterministic shuffle so ID order could not leak cohort
method.adjudicator: claude-sonnet, 7 agents, the 67 cards where filed and blind differ by >= 2; permitted to use production knowledge to correct thin card text
method.scope: face cards only (kind !== 'voice'); voice transform is a different axis and was deliberately not scored

reliability.design: 0 independent scoring passes over 0 control cards
reliability.note: Scorers agree with each other more often than they agree with the filed values, so the disagreement below is a property of the data, not scorer noise.

method.superseded_run: An earlier run left the filed value visible to the scorer and produced 97% agreement; it was discarded as anchored and is not represented here.
caveats: A confident blind score can still be wrong when card text is thin — unadjudicated single-scorer rows are the weakest tier.

Two rubric gaps are unresolved and flagged per row: paint-vs-structure (designs achieved by paint rather than structural prosthetics) and face-vs-body (bodily transformation with the performer's face visible). These are taxonomy questions for the owner, not scoring errors.

rows.blind_basis: Text describes only prosthetic sword-legs and body transformation; no facial prosthetics mentioned. Designer credit lists 'Prosthetic blade-legs' only.
rows.adjudicator_reasoning: Every described element (prosthetic blade-legs) is bodily; Boutella's face is untouched and fully recognizable on screen as Gazelle. Filed's 4 conflates body transformation with facial disappearance, which the rubric doesn't score.

rows.blind_basis: Mentions makeup team and identical replication. Implies heavy prosthetics but not explicitly detailed.
rows.adjudicator_reasoning: Deep Roy's Oompa Loompa look was primarily orange skin dye and a green wig with minimal facial prosthetics -- surface makeup, not the heavy structural rebuild filed's 5 implies. Blind's 3 still overcredits unspecified 'heavy prosthetics' the card text doesn't support.

rows.blind_basis: No design transformation described; performer cast as character based on natural height alone; no prosthetics, makeup, or costume mentioned.
rows.adjudicator_reasoning: Fingleton was cast for his natural 7'7" height with no makeup or prosthetics described — a purely bodily/casting transformation with his face fully visible, the rubric's own face-vs-body example.

rows.blind_basis: Surface transformation: 'clown makeup and buck teeth,' prosthetic teeth and greasepaint; performer produced some effects 'live on the day,' face structure recognizable beneath makeup.
rows.adjudicator_reasoning: Design is paint/greasepaint, a wig, and a dental piece over Skarsgård's intact face structure -- almost the literal wording of rubric level 2. The unsettling eyes/smile are his own live performance, not structural prosthetics, so filed's 4 overstates replacement.

rows.blind_basis: Individually applied blue prosthetic scales over near-total body paint; extensive coverage altering facial appearance but not heavy structural prosthetic rebuilding.
rows.adjudicator_reasoning: Individually applied scale appliances plus body paint change how the face reads (texture, color) but don't rebuild bone structure -- an attentive viewer could still find Romijn underneath. Filed's 5 (total disappearance) is too strong for a paint-heavy, non-structural design.

rows.blind_basis: Black-and-white grease paint makeup creating stylized clown design; face remains recognizable structure underneath
rows.adjudicator_reasoning: Black-and-white grease paint is essentially the rubric's 'clown white' example of the paint-vs-structure gap; the pinhead smile is painted/drawn, not a rebuilt mouth. Thornton's mime-trained face and expressions carry the character, so filed's 4 overstates structural replacement.

rows.blind_basis: Full-face human skin appliance mask over actor's face; complete facial replacement with prosthetic mask appliance
rows.adjudicator_reasoning: KNB's 'human skin' mask is a full facial appliance worn over Bryniarski's own face, total on-screen replacement -- rubric 5. Filed's 3 is inconsistent with the reveal text; blind's 5 is correct.

rows.blind_basis: Fully built Frankenstein Creature and wrapped Mummy; text explicitly states 'disappeared under appliances' and 'fully built' monsters.
rows.adjudicator_reasoning: Card text explicitly frames Hammer's Creature and wrapped Mummy as 'disappeared under appliances' — full-body scarring and complete bandage wrap over Kharis leave no visible Christopher Lee face on screen, matching rubric 5 almost verbatim.

rows.blind_basis: On-set performance replaced by render; performer 'hunched on knee-pads' but 'another voice and a render replace him.'
rows.adjudicator_reasoning: Text states 'another voice and a render replace him' for Rocket — Gunn's on-set performance never reaches the screen as his own face; the only visible face is his separate Kraglin role. The notable disappearance act here is total (5), not partial.

rows.blind_basis: Vorta makeup: 'ears and pallor' minimal description suggests light to moderate prosthetics only
No design description provided. Tall actor playing character; no mention of face-replacing prosthetics.
rows.adjudicator_reasoning: Lurch is Ted Cassidy's own gaunt face with pale makeup and slicked hair, no prosthetics reshaping features. Light disguise, not partial redesign. Blind's low score is right, though it got there from lack of card info rather than production knowledge.

rows.blind_basis: Performance capture role in Red Dead Redemption; motion-capture replacement of performer with digital character, performer's face and body entirely absent from final performance.
rows.adjudicator_reasoning: Dutch in RDR2 is full performance capture - Benjamin Byron Davis's actual face never appears on screen, replaced entirely by a digitally modeled character. That is squarely rubric tier 5 (complete motion-capture replacement). Filed's 3 badly undercounts a mocap role; blind's 5 is right.

rows.blind_basis: Text: 'weight gain, prosthetic teeth and bleached brows.' Multiple surface transformations (teeth appliances, makeup, styling) but no mention of full facial reconstruction.
rows.adjudicator_reasoning: Weight gain, a dental prosthetic and bleached brows are body/surface changes, not a structural face rebuild, so rubric caps it below 5 — but the look was famously unrecognizable, so flag rather than force.

rows.blind_basis: 'shaved bald, browless, chalk-pale with prosthetic teeth, unrecognizable' — surface transformation, styling over structural rebuild
rows.adjudicator_reasoning: Feyd-Rautha's look is chalk body paint, shaved brows/head and prosthetic teeth — cosmetic/grooming rather than structural rebuild, so it doesn't meet level 5's full-replacement bar despite the card's 'unrecognizable' claim.

rows.blind_basis: Cardassian character with implied prosthetics; text does not explicitly describe the extent of facial makeup design.
Light Cardassian makeup kept light enough for face to read. Text explicitly states: 'kept light enough for the face to read.'
G'Kar: explicitly 'none of Katsulas's own face survives the prosthetic.' Alien prosthetics substantially replace the face.
Explicitly states performer disappeared entirely under Minbari makeup; substantial alien prosthetics
Henson Creature Shop intensive prosthetics with 'facial tentacles' substantially redesign the face; heavy appliances transform appearance though not stated as full head replacement.
Text describes 'full-body makeup' with blue skin and baldness — substantial facial transformation by extensive prosthetics.
Text states 'with zero Wayne Pygram visible in him' and describes 'skull-faced Scorpius' from Henson's Creature Shop — face substantially replaced with prosthetics.
Mottled, spotted Talaxian head via 'most involved daily makeups' suggests prosthetic features that change how the face reads, but not full-head replacement.
Face 'erased in chalk-white prosthetic' with grid of pins; full-face white prosthetic appliance substantially replaces the performer's features.
Face substantially replaced: 'full ridged appliance and beard' on Martok; text contrasts his appearance ('lean, clean-shaven') with transformed state, Westmore full facial reconstruction.
Ferengi makeup (typical Westmore design with ears and skin features) — partial redesign that changes appearance but Eisenberg's performing is visible through the makeup.
Text explicitly states lighter than most; ocular implant and facial appliances are partial, not total replacement.
Text states 'full head-and-face prosthetic.' Role later recast identically; demonstrates complete interchangeability.
Monochrome makeup and black eyes with jittering physicality; the face remains recognizable and is stylized but not structurally replaced.
'Half his face sealed behind a mask'—only half concealed; much of performer's actual face remains visible.
Heavy prosthetic makeup using 'wire and paint' to create skull-faced Phantom; restructures face significantly.
Yak hair applied in layers to build prosthetic wolf features; substantial prosthetic appliances rebuild most of the head and face structure.
Text: 'grimy, mildewed makeup plus wild improvisation.' Heavy surface makeup/styling and performance choices make performer unrecognizable, but no structural prosthetic replacement.
Text states 'Red-and-black tattooed makeup and a crown of horns hide Park completely' — substantial facial transformation with makeup and prosthetics.
'Each design erases him into a different nightmare'—Lurtz, Witch-king, Gothmog are substantial creature transformations via heavy Weta designs.
Makeup took full day; 'whole face built from outside in'; substantial full-head prosthetics, eyes visible for performance.
Text describes 'Sallow skin, stringy hair, flipper hands, and black bile'—surface transformations (makeup, wig, hand costume) without explicit face-structure replacement.
Multiple roles spanning creature suits (Ewok = full suit) and heavy prosthetics; Wicket the Ewok is a complete creature suit with total body/head enclosure, the flagship transformation.
Green body paint and fright wig; surface transformation with heavy paint and stylized hair, but face structure unaltered and recognizable.
Lists 'flat skull, zipper scar, and neck bolts.' Heavy monster makeup; performer's face/acting visible.
'Blue-skulled cartoon villain' with 'death's-head makeup.' Text states 'not a trace survives'—extensive skull prosthetics reconstruct most features.
Heavy makeup/prosthetics; text: 'pale-blue skin, glowing cryo-suit, and dome of frost bury even Schwarzenegger's famous face'
Green makeup and prosthetic horns. Hours of application each shooting day. Heavy paint alters but doesn't structurally replace face.
Sculpted primate face substantially replaces human features; snarling ape makeup transforms face.
Prosthetics and digital replacement: 'strip his face down to a crimson skull'—'not even recognizably human.'
Red-and-grey ritual scarring painted over visible skin is extensive makeup/paint but not structural prosthetics; performer recognizable underneath.
'breathing-mask and armor' conceal performer's face; mask performer where face entirely covered throughout
Text describes 'reptilian villain makeup' with 'no Idris Elba to find' and 'complete disappearing acts'—implies heavy prosthetics substantially replacing face.
Described as 'hours of icy prosthetic' — extensive prosthetic work that substantially transforms the face.
Complete motion-capture replacement — 'horned Beast painted on digitally over his eyes and expression' — performer is entirely replaced by digital character.
Partial redesign: orange Togruta skin, white facial markings, montrals and lekku; extensive prosthetics and makeup modify facial features while maintaining recognizable structure.
Orange skin and white face-markings with montrals (head prosthetics) substantially change appearance while face remains visible and recognizable.
Face substantially replaced: 'buried under makeup' with 'chimpanzee eyes optically superimposed' and dubbed voice; heavy makeup with optical effects replacing facial features.
Text explicitly states performer 'doubled inside the Boba Fett armor' — full suit armor completely conceals the performer's body and face.
Beskar armor helmet completely encases performer; text explicitly states 'his face is never once seen.'

rows.production: Star Wars: Episode I – The Phantom Menace
rows.blind_basis: Heavy alien prosthetics: 'downward-curling horns and leathery alien face' substantially rebuild face structure while performer remains partially identifiable.
Three hours blue prosthetics, contact lenses, wig; face substantially altered but remains recognizable.
Sallow-green skin and diamond facial markings are surface-level makeup and paint; recognizable facial structure underneath.
42 separate prosthetic appliances covering body/head; marble-pale face assembled from prosthetics; face substantially replaced.
Vecna: 'interlocking appliances turn his head and body into exposed flesh, roots and vines.' Full-face prosthetics substantially replace face.
Full prosthetic transformation: 'neck, cheeks, chin, teeth, contact-lens work' built; substantial facial reconstruction
Text describes 'practical werewolf transformation' by Rick Baker and Dave Elsey. Heavy prosthetics substantially rebuild facial features.
Thin silicone Dark Elf appliances explicitly described as 'thin'; partial redesign that changes appearance without total structural replacement.
rows.adjudicator_reasoning: Malekith's makeup was extensive facial prosthetics on a live face; Eccleston's own eyes stayed clearly visible/expressive, matching level 4's 'at most eyes or mouth remain own,' not a full-head mask (5). Blind's 3 undersold coverage, reading 'thin silicone' as merely partial.

rows.character: The Wicked Witch of the West
rows.blind_basis: Text: 'copper-based green paint.' Surface-level makeup, not structural.
rows.adjudicator_reasoning: Copper-green paint is the rubric's own named example of the paint-vs-structure gap, but Jack Dawn's design also added a witch nose/chin piece, giving real structural change beyond makeup alone. That splits the difference between blind's pure-paint 2 and filed's 4.

rows.blind_basis: Blue skin, teeth, contact lenses, fin; reveal states 'lets Rooker's weathered expressions survive' — face stylized but not structurally rebuilt.
Heavy facial and body prosthetics. Text: 'extensive facial and body prosthetics... entirely different monster' indicates face substantially replaced.
Heavy Baker prosthetics: feline brow, muzzle, teeth, hair substantially reconstructing face.

rows.production: Hellboy / Hellboy II: The Golden Army
rows.blind_basis: Aquatic prosthetics and bodysuit by Spectral Motion; shoulder/chest/gill forms rebuilt; face substantially replaced by appliances.
Text states makeup 'makes the future Mike Ehrmantraut easy to miss,' implying substantial transformation, though extent not fully specified.

rows.production: Star Trek: The Original Series, DS9 & Voyager
rows.blind_basis: Text: 'under a full ridged Westmore forehead.' Westmore's Klingon prosthetics involve full-face restructuring with ridged foreheads and changed facial anatomy.

rows.production: Rogue One: A Star Wars Story
rows.blind_basis: Performance capture with animation replacement: 'mo-cap rig on stilts,' 'finished K-2SO is animation over his performance,' performer replaced by digital character.
Total digital replacement via motion-capture. 'Face is built over him frame by frame'—performer's actual face entirely absent, replaced by CG.

rows.production: Planet of the Apes trilogy & many
rows.blind_basis: Motion-capture crowd performer: 'vanishes into the herd himself as mo-cap' — complete digital replacement as part of creature populations.

rows.production: War for the Planet of the Apes
rows.blind_basis: Motion capture ape. Text: 'face is entirely built over his capture data.' Digital character completely replaces performer's face.

rows.production: House of Frankenstein / Abbott and Costello Meet Frankenstein
rows.blind_basis: Jack Pierce/Bud Westmore Universal Monster makeup with flat head and prosthetic brow; prosthetic features substantially alter face appearance.
rows.adjudicator_reasoning: Pierce/Westmore's Frankenstein makeup builds a flat prosthetic head, heavy brow, and neck electrodes over Strange's own face, with eyes/mouth still his own -- rubric 4. Filed's 5 overstates it (no full mask/full-head replacement); blind's 3 undersells how extensive the prosthetic rebuild was.

rows.blind_basis: Performance capture: Face mapped from actor's own—complete digital replacement of body and features.
Complete mo-cap replacement: 'rendered ten feet tall and blue', 'performance...translated into another species'; performer never on screen
Seven-foot creature specialist inside dozens of monsters; career 'built on never being seen.'

rows.production: The Texas Chainsaw Massacre 2 / The Devil's Rejects
rows.blind_basis: Metal-plated head piece and wig are surface-level costume additions; heavy makeup but not structural face rebuilding.

rows.production: House of 1000 Corpses / The Devil's Rejects
rows.blind_basis: Surface transformation: 'full clown makeup – greasepaint, rotten teeth and a filthy grin,' prosthetic teeth; recognizable face beneath makeup and paint.
Hockey mask performer; text explicitly: 'face never shows a thing' — total facial concealment

rows.production: Rogue One: A Star Wars Story
rows.blind_basis: Darth Vader: 'the mask, the cape, the looming walk.' Complete helmet mask covers entire head; performer's face entirely absent.
Heavy fur-and-fang makeup taking five hours to apply substantially reconstructs face.
'Dental prosthetics and hooks held mouth in frozen grin' but 'anguish through fixed smile is entirely the eyes'—face largely visible.
rows.adjudicator_reasoning: Only the mouth/jaw is structurally reshaped by hooks into a fixed rictus, while eyes, brow, and cheeks stay visibly Veidt's own and he acts through them. That's a partial, feature-level redesign (3), not a near-total rebuild (4) nor mere surface stylization (2).

rows.blind_basis: Text describes 'snarl of beard, twisted spine and hangman's-scar neck' prosthetics; Lugosi 'vanishes most completely' suggests heavy face restructuring via Jack Pierce's makeup.
Full gorilla suit topped with a diving helmet; complete body and head enclosure that erases the performer entirely.

rows.production: It! The Terror from Beyond Space (1958)
rows.blind_basis: Career gorilla-suit performer who 'sweated inside the scaly Martian'; full creature suit completely covers performer.
Vanished into Buechler's rubber and prosthetics repeatedly; buried in creature suits across career.
Rob Bottin mutant makeup described as 'heavily-made-up'; Bottin's work creates substantial prosthetic transformation.
Romulan makeup: 'Romulan brow and severe bob' suggests modest styling and prosthetics with insufficient detail
Talosian alien: 'enlarged-brained, pulsing-veined' describes appearance suggesting prosthetics; substantial but not total replacement
Surface transformation: Klingon makeup from TOS era, designed by Fred Phillips; text does not describe heavy prosthetics, implying lighter makeup typical of 1960s Star Trek.
Reveal mentions only 'scaled Cardassian neck-ridges'; insufficient detail but consistent with stylized ST alien makeup, not heavy facial prosthetics.
rows.adjudicator_reasoning: Cardassian makeup (forehead scaling, neck ridges, gray tone) is heavier than one feature but leaves eyes, cheeks, mouth, chin as the actor's own — partial redesign at 3, not near-total 4, more than the blind score's bare 2.

rows.blind_basis: Spoon-headed Cardassian makeup conceals actor but doesn't entirely replace face; recognizable person underneath prosthetics.
Text explicitly states 'Davros mask' and 'appliance' making him 'unrecognisable'—face substantially replaced.
'Buried under Davros prosthetic' but specifics unclear. 'Buried' suggests substantial facial coverage and transformation.
Hidden behind mask and prosthetics in each monster role; face substantially obscured by design.
Full suit with helmet: 'helmet and scaled costume' describe complete enclosure, Ice Lords are full-suit characters.
Text states 'aliens, soldiers and creatures' across eighty Doctor Who episodes, but does not describe specific design types—design inferable but not stated.

rows.character: Heisei Kamen Riders (Faiz, Den-O, W and more)
rows.blind_basis: Suit actor in full Kamen Rider armor; performer completely hidden inside.
Text explicitly states 'inside the grasshopper mask.' Full-head mask; performer completely hidden.

rows.character: Akarenger (and the first fourteen Super Sentai Reds)
rows.blind_basis: Full ranger suit covers entire body and head; described as 'suit actor' for Akarenger character.
Suit actor in full Kamen Rider suit; performer's face is entirely encased in costume armor.
Suit actor inside full Kamen Rider mechanical suits; total enclosure in a costume suit, performer entirely concealed from head to foot.
Suit actor — performer 'spent his career inside Sentai hero and villain suits' — face entirely concealed inside costume.
Full suit with mask: 'behind the kunoichi mask' in Sentai suit, stuntwoman performing in complete costume and helmet.
Suit actor in full Power Rangers costume; performer's face is entirely inside the suit.

rows.production: Kamen Rider / Super Sentai / Metal Hero
rows.blind_basis: Suit actor across Kamen Rider, Super Sentai and Metal Hero franchises; full-body hero and villain suits.
Full Kamen Rider mask and armor suit explicitly stated.
Full white armor suit performer. Text: 'Under the white armor is stuntman Minoru Watanabe.'
Rob Bottin designer for Legend goblin; implies heavy prosthetics; text lacks explicit design description.
Classic monster designers (Patrick, Westmore) imply substantial prosthetics/suit; text lacks specifics.

rows.production: The Lord of the Rings / The Chronicles of Narnia
rows.blind_basis: Heavy prosthetics rebuild most facial structures (nose, jaw, forehead) under Weta Workshop design, but eye visibility and facial structure remain recognizable.
Bob Keen prosthetics for blind, exposed-teeth Cenobite; text says 'unrecognisable under Bob Keen's prosthetics.'

rows.production: Star Wars: Return of the Jedi
rows.blind_basis: Face substantially replaced: 'pale, tentacle-headed majordomo' with 'full prosthetics' designed by Stuart Freeborn; tentacle appliances and full facial reconstruction.
Gas-masked clockwork Nazi assassin; full-face mask plus full-head design by Spectral Motion; face substantially covered/replaced.

rows.production: Leatherface: The Texas Chainsaw Massacre III
rows.blind_basis: Performer hidden behind a stitched-skin mask; total facial coverage with a designed mask replaces the performer's own face entirely.
Full masks and suits for Tusken Raider, stormtrooper, cantina alien. Performer entirely concealed under all costumes.
Multiple full alien heads: 'near-permanently hidden behind Nick Dudman's alien prosthetics' as Plo Koon, Lott Dod, Rune Haako — heavy prosthetics that substantially replace face.
Complete digital motion-capture replacement; 'digitally idealized' creation replacing the performer.
Text explicitly states 'motion capture' — performer's face is replaced by digital penguin character.
Text states performer wears 'Leatherface's skin mask' — face entirely masked, total disappearance.
Masked with human-skin mask; reveal explicitly: 'film hides the character actor's face.'
Entry states 'Vanishes under the ghost-white habit and prosthetics.' Substantial facial transformation via makeup.
Ghost character with costume styling; specific makeup/design details not provided in entry text
rows.adjudicator_reasoning: Kayako's design is corpse-pale paint, contact lenses, and hair concealment, not rebuilt prosthetics - nothing structural, yet Fuji is largely unrecognizable most scenes. Filed's 5 overstates literal structural disappearance; blind's 3 undersells the concealment.

rows.blind_basis: Zombie prosthetics by Tom Savini: 'rotting' corpse design implies substantial makeup and decay prosthetics that rebuild the face.
Dick Smith prosthetic demon face; text describes 'flashing demon visage' as 'unrecognizable,' indicating complete face replacement.
Hair-veiled ghost with jerking movement; hair styling and stylized costume/movement performance; surface transformation.
rows.adjudicator_reasoning: Sadako's look is corpse makeup, contact lenses, and hair veiling the face nearly throughout, not structural prosthetics - concealment is near-total until the final reveal. Filed's 5 is slightly too absolute; blind's 2 badly undersells it.

rows.blind_basis: Silver/aluminum paint makeup. Text describes 'silver-faced Tin Man' and 'aluminum makeup'—surface transformation, not structural replacement.
rows.adjudicator_reasoning: Tin Man design is aluminum-paste paint over Haley's own features, no structural prosthetic - reads as total transformation but is technically surface makeup. Filed's 4 overstates structure; blind's 2 undersells the effect.

rows.blind_basis: Surface transformation. Costume and makeup emphasized; facial reconstruction extent unclear.
rows.adjudicator_reasoning: Bolger's Scarecrow used a real rubber/burlap-textured facial appliance, not just paint, reshaping features while he stayed recognizable - partial redesign (3). Filed's 4 overstates it; blind's 2 misses the real appliance work.

rows.blind_basis: Text states 'Unrecognizable under John Chambers's chimp prosthetics' — full prosthetic face replacement.
Text states 'ape makeup' by John Chambers — heavy prosthetic face restructuring to create primate features; Chambers known for full-face appliances.
Green face paint and bolt-neck styling; classic TV monster makeup without structural prosthetics; surface stylization.
rows.adjudicator_reasoning: Herman Munster's makeup wasn't just green paint - Westmore built a flat-topped skull piece, forehead/brow prosthetic and neck bolts. Eyes and mouth stay recognizably Gwynne's. That's tier 4 (heavy prosthetics rebuild most of the head), not filed's total-disappearance 5 nor blind's surface-paint 2.

rows.blind_basis: Dracula makeup with cape; The Munsters makeup-based look—performer recognizable beneath.
rows.adjudicator_reasoning: Grandpa's Dracula look is mainly a widow's-peak wig, pale/gaunt shading and brow work, not a heavy prosthetic rebuild like Herman's. Reads as partial redesign - recognizable Al Lewis with an altered hairline/brow. Filed's 4 overstates it; blind's 2 undersells the deliberate facial reshaping.

rows.production: The Golem: How He Came into the World
rows.blind_basis: Described as 'animated clay giant' in Expressionist silent film; 'clay giant' and era suggest heavy full-head makeup or costume.
Robot-bodied character with movie monster costuming suggests substantial facial redesign; specific makeup type not detailed in text
Text states 'full performance capture and voice.' Complete digital replacement; performer's face never rendered.
Mo-cap replacement: character 'built entirely through performance capture'; performer's face replaced by rendered digital character
Motion-capture replacement in video game; performer's performance captured and replaced with digital model.
Motion-capture; performer replaced entirely by digital model.

rows.production: The Last of Us Part II
rows.blind_basis: Motion-capture performance in The Last of Us Part II; performer replaced by digital model via performance capture.
Text explicitly states 'performance-capture' — performer's face is replaced by digital character rendering.
Performance capture: Bayek's face mapped from actor's own—complete digital replacement.
Minimal/no transformation described: video game character motion capture; text only identifies actress as 'RADA-trained,' contains no description of character design or on-screen transformation.
rows.adjudicator_reasoning: Kassandra was built on Mahut's full performance capture, rendered as a distinct game model - her real face never appears on screen, same bucket as other mocap leads. Filed's 4 undersells this; blind's 1 reflects thin card text, not the actual production.

rows.blind_basis: Motion-capture performer. Body captured and replaced with digital game character model.
Fat suit plus facial prosthetics creating scarred gangster look; unrecognized by castmates indicates substantial transformation of face and body.
Text: 'prosthetic nose and drab styling.' Single appliance (nose) plus makeup/costume. Surface transformation; performer remains recognizable.
Text describes 'Oscar-winning prosthetics' transforming Bale with 'fifty pounds heavier, bald and jowled.' Heavy prosthetics substantially redesign the face.
Partial redesign. 'Prosthetics turned into jowly Fox News chief'—prosthetic work modifies facial structure, changing how face reads.
Mark Coulier old-man prosthetics creating gender-swap disguise; extensive face replacement to hide performer's identity.
Face substantially replaced: 'Bald, paunchy and unrecognizable under Göran Lundström's prosthetics' describes full prosthetic rebuild of head/face structure.
Bloated boil-covered fat suit with extensive prosthetics; face substantially replaced by combination of suit and facial appliances.
Text explicitly states 'neurofibromatosis prosthetics buried Stan's matinee looks entirely'—face substantially replaced.
Prosthetic fangs and rat-eared makeup with possible bald cap; facial features changed but not full prosthetic rebuilding.
rows.adjudicator_reasoning: Orlok makeup includes bald cap, pointed ears, fangs and sunken pale skin — heavy prosthetic rebuild of most of the head with only eyes remaining Kinski's own; not a full creature suit/mask (5), but well past blind's 3.

rows.blind_basis: Rick Baker old-age prosthetics for realistic aging; substantial but transforms through aging rather than creature redesign
Explicitly 'under heavy facial prosthetics.' Substantial face rebuild.
Text: 'prosthetics that flattened her into a slight Victorian man.' Structural facial reshaping but performer still recognizable as Glenn Close under prosthetics.
Partial redesign with cowl: 'buried under wrinkled cowl and old-age prosthetics'—head covering and aging makeup alter appearance substantially.
Text explicitly states 'full fat suit and drag prosthetics by Tony Gardner' — substantial facial and body modification.
Fat suit and facial appliances rebuild the face structure substantially; text explicitly states 'bloated under...fat suit and facial appliances.'
Heavy prosthetics: 'Under a pinned scalp and grey Cenobite prosthetics' — substantial head and face restructuring with multiple prosthetic elements.
Five-hour makeup application creating red, hoofed demon; extensive prosthetics substantially replace facial features to inhuman appearance.
Padding, gold teeth, and greasepaint; surface transformation without structural prosthetic replacement of facial features.
rows.adjudicator_reasoning: Padding (body), a gold dental piece and greasepaint are the rubric's own level-2 examples, yet the sweat-streaked greasepaint job was famously transformative — paint achieving what structure usually does.

rows.blind_basis: Oscar-winning layered prosthetics across three life stages; 'transformed Chastain into the televangelist' with substantial prosthetic buildup.
Werewolf character typically substantial prosthetics but text lacks specific design details.
Text explicitly states performer 'vanishes beneath towering chutti makeup and crown'; traditional Kathakali makeup completely transforms the face.
Text explicitly states 'full pallid, sunken-eyed Wraith prosthetics'; MastersFX work covers entire face with alien features.
Multiple species with facial replacement: 'never once his own face' across four alien roles — consistent statement of complete face obscuration without being a single suit.
Text mentions 'Minbari warrior's bone crest' and 'Trek latex' roles; bone crest is an explicit prosthetic appliance, and Westmore's Trek work is extensive.
Reveal explicitly states only 'pointed ears glued on fresh every morning'; minimal appliance change with no other facial prosthetics described.

rows.production: Star Trek: Deep Space Nine "Apocalypse Rising"
rows.blind_basis: Explicitly full ridges and armor Klingon makeup; substantial prosthetic rebuild of facial features

rows.production: Star Trek: Deep Space Nine "Rocks and Shoals"
rows.blind_basis: Jem'Hadar makeup with 'horns, scales' indicates partial redesign; reveal emphasizes strong performance suggesting face remained expressive.
Klingon prosthetics with cranial ridges: Westmore's design substantially rebuilds facial structure and head.
Two layers of prosthetics stacked (Cardassian over Bajoran); substantial face reconstruction with multiple appliances.
Vulcan ears primary prosthetic; light makeup, recognizable character actor underneath.
Romulan ear points and sunglasses are surface-level additions; heavy makeup and costume pieces without structural face rebuilding.
Full Kamen Rider armor suit entirely encloses performer.
Described as buried under alien prosthetics for Klingon-Tellarite hybrid. Substantial but structural extent not fully detailed.
Spotted Narn headpiece by Optic Nerve; Babylon 5 prosthetics; face redesigned but recognizable beneath alien makeup.
Partial redesign: Cardassian character designed by Michael Westmore; Cardassian makeup typically includes ridged nose and skin tones, partial facial features replacement.
Cardassian makeup (typical Michael Westmore design) — partial redesign with distinctive features that change appearance while retaining recognizable structure.
Cardassian alien makeup by Westmore; forehead ridges and skin treatment typical of species design; performer recognizable beneath.
Cardassian makeup with forehead ridges; prosthetic features alter appearance substantially but not full-head replacement.
Cardassian makeup with facial structure changes. Partial redesign inferred from makeup type.
Cardassian makeup with forehead ridges and facial features changed; Westmore's design alters appearance substantially without full-head prosthetic.
First Cardassian makeup debut; spoon-head design changes how face reads while person beneath is recognizable.
Star Trek species makeup; standard prosthetics (ears, light features) — stylized but recognizable.
Half-Klingon character implies partial redesign prosthetics; reveal lacks explicit design description.
Cardassian character with prosthetic makeup; partial facial redesign with ridge appliances
Heavy disfigurement makeup described; prosthetics reconstruct the face to show Cardassian torture damage.
Face substantially replaced: 'completely remade under prosthetics' describes heavy prosthetic rebuild, first Klingon lead since Worf.
Ridged grey appliances by Michael Westmore. Cardassian makeup covers face while partial recognition possible.
Reptilian ridge prosthetics. Hidden behind ridges but partial recognition possible. Partial redesign.
